structured_choices.data_source: Electronic health records (EHR) from a large urban hospital spanning five years (60 months). Approximately 50,000 patient records containing structured data (age, sex, comorbidities, admission type, length of stay, discharge medications) and unstructured data (patient-reported outcomes from standardized post-discharge surveys administered within 48 hours of discharge). All data linked to 30-day readmission outcome (binary: readmitted yes/no).
structured_choices.label_definition: Binary label: readmission = 1 if patient presents to the same hospital within 30 days of discharge for any reason; readmission = 0 if no return visit within 30 days. Outcome status is determined from administrative billing records (ground truth) and locked 30 days post-discharge. Expected label prevalence: approximately 15–20% based on typical urban hospital readmission rates.
structured_choices.split_method: Temporal split with stratification by readmission status within each time window. Train: months 1–36 (n ≈ 30,000). Validation: months 37–48 (n ≈ 10,000). Test: months 49–60 (n ≈ 10,000). Temporal split is essential because (1) RNN models exploit temporal sequences; using future data for training constitutes information leakage; (2) hospital processes, community resources, and discharge practices evolve over time, and test set performance on held-out future months reflects realistic deployment conditions; (3) patient-reported outcomes collected post-discharge should not inform predictions for earlier patients. Stratification within each window ensures readmission classes are represented proportionally in all splits, preventing validation/test sets from becoming unbalanced due to temporal clustering of readmissions.
structured_choices.preprocessing_fit_point: All preprocessing transformers (scaling, imputation, encoding) are fit exclusively on the training set (months 1–36). Fitted transformers are then applied to validation (months 37–48) and test (months 49–60) sets without refitting. This prevents data leakage from future data into the training procedure. For missing values in structured features: mean imputation is fit on train; for patient-reported outcomes (unstructured text or survey responses), missing values are handled via indicator variables ('missingness' is retained as a feature, as missingness itself may be informative). No look-ahead imputation is performed.
structured_choices.features: Structured features: age, sex, race/ethnicity, comorbidity count (Charlson score), admission type (emergency/planned), length of stay, discharge disposition, primary diagnosis (ICD code embedding via pre-trained clinical BERT), count of discharge medications, and prior 12-month readmission history. Unstructured features derived from patient-reported outcomes: raw survey responses (e.g., 'I feel confident managing my condition at home') are embedded using a fine-tuned clinical domain BERT model into fixed-length vectors (768 dimensions). RNN input: sequence of (structured features + embedded outcomes) at discharge and at three post-discharge follow-up survey timepoints (7 days, 14 days, 21 days post-discharge if surveys were completed). Sequences are padded/truncated to length 4. No feature constructed from the readmission label; no future information (post-discharge events) incorporated into features.
structured_choices.model: Bidirectional LSTM (BiLSTM) with two stacked layers (128 and 64 units), applied to sequences of features. Architecture: embed all structured features (non-sequential) as a static context vector (dense layer, 32 units); concatenate static context with sequential outcomes at each timestep; pass concatenated sequence through BiLSTM layers; apply attention mechanism over BiLSTM outputs; feed attention-weighted output to final dense layer (64 units, ReLU) and sigmoid output for binary classification. Hyperparameters tuned via grid search on validation set: learning rate ∈ {0.0001, 0.0005, 0.001}, dropout ∈ {0.2, 0.4}, batch size ∈ {32, 64}. Training uses early stopping (patience=5 epochs) on validation AUROC.
structured_choices.baseline: Logistic regression trained on the same structured feature set (demographics, clinical history, comorbidities, prior readmission, diagnosis embedding) without the sequential patient-reported outcomes. The baseline does not use temporal sequences—it uses only the discharge-time snapshot of structured data. The baseline receives equivalent preprocessing (fit on train, applied to val/test), equivalent hyperparameter tuning effort (grid search over regularization strength L2 penalty ∈ {0.001, 0.01, 0.1, 1.0} on validation set), and is evaluated on identical train/val/test splits. This ensures the comparison isolates the contribution of RNN architecture and sequential patient-reported outcomes, versus a standard clinical prediction approach. The baseline is fair because it uses the best available structured features but omits the sequential signal that the RNN can exploit.
structured_choices.primary_metric: Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve (AUROC). Justification: Readmission prediction is a low-prevalence classification problem (≈15–20% readmission rate). Accuracy is misleading (a model predicting all 'no readmission' achieves ≈80–85% accuracy). AUROC is threshold-agnostic and reflects the model's ability to rank-order patients by readmission risk across all decision thresholds. This aligns with the stakeholder objective: intervention programs require a risk ranking to prioritize high-risk patients for intensive case management, not a single hard threshold. AUROC allows stakeholders to set their own operating point (sensitivity/specificity trade-off) based on resource availability and acceptable false-positive rate.
secondary_metrics: Precision-Recall AUC (PR-AUC): complements AUROC in imbalanced settings; reflects model's precision at varying recall levels, useful if stakeholders want to understand the cost of false positives.
Sensitivity at 80% specificity: clinically interpretable threshold; reflects true positive rate when false positive rate is held to 20%, informing intervention feasibility.
Calibration (Brier score, calibration slope): measures whether predicted probabilities reflect true readmission frequencies; important if predicted risk scores are communicated to clinicians.
Stratified performance by admission type and primary diagnosis: detects whether model generalizes equally across patient subgroups or exhibits bias.
structured_choices.model_selection_data: Hyperparameters for both RNN and baseline logistic regression are selected using the validation set (months 37–48). Grid search is performed: for each hyperparameter configuration, the model is trained on the training set and evaluated on the validation set using AUROC. The configuration with the highest validation AUROC is selected. The test set (months 49–60) is held out and used exactly once: after model selection is complete, the final model (with tuned hyperparameters) is evaluated on the test set to produce the reported performance metrics. The test set is never used for hyperparameter tuning, threshold selection, or any other optimization step.
structured_choices.test_set_policy: The test set is locked and untouched during all development, hyperparameter tuning, and model selection phases. After the final model (RNN and baseline) are fully trained and tuned on the combined train+validation data (refitted on train+validation after selection to maximize training signal), the test set is used in a single-pass evaluation. Test set predictions and metrics are recorded but not used to adjust the model or retrain. This ensures the test set represents the true held-out generalization performance on future (temporally later) data.
structured_choices.confound_controls: Known confound: Variability in hospital discharge processes and community support resources influence readmission independently of the model. Mitigation strategies: (1) Stratified analysis: performance is evaluated separately for subgroups defined by discharge disposition (discharged home, discharged to skilled nursing facility, discharged to rehabilitation) and by neighborhood-level social deprivation index (if available in EHR). This reveals whether the model's advantage (RNN vs. logistic regression) persists across discharge contexts. (2) Temporal stability check: performance is evaluated on three non-overlapping test sub-windows (months 49–54, 55–57, 58–60) to detect whether model performance degrades as discharge practices or community resources shift. (3) Feature importance analysis: post-hoc inspection of RNN attention weights to verify that learned sequences prioritize clinically meaningful outcomes (e.g., medication adherence, symptom monitoring) rather than proxies for unmeasured confounds. (4) Documentation of limitations: the experiment explicitly acknowledges that superior predictive performance does NOT establish causal mechanisms; observed readmission differences may be driven by discharge processes or support availability. The model predicts risk, but causality requires additional intervention studies. Scope: This experiment establishes whether RNNs with patient-reported outcomes improve risk stratification (predictive validity) on held-out future data compared to structured-only logistic regression. It does NOT establish (a) causal effects of patient-reported outcomes on readmission, (b) whether the model's predictions are actionable, or (c) whether deployment will reduce readmissions (requires an intervention trial).

design_narrative:
## Experiment Design: RNN vs. Logistic Regression for Hospital Readmission Prediction

### 1. Data Strategy

We will source data from five years of electronic health records (months 1–60) from a large urban hospital, comprising approximately 50,000 patient records. The dataset includes structured clinical data (age, sex, comorbidities, admission type, length of stay, discharge disposition, discharge medications, primary diagnosis) and unstructured patient-reported outcomes (surveys administered within 48 hours of discharge asking about symptom management, medication adherence, confidence in self-care, and access to follow-up appointments). The binary outcome is 30-day readmission status, determined from administrative billing records locked 30 days post-discharge. Expected readmission prevalence is 15–20%, typical for urban hospital populations.

Data quality considerations: Unstructured patient-reported outcomes may have missing responses due to patient non-completion or language barriers. We will retain missingness as a feature (indicator variables) rather than imputing, as absence of response may itself correlate with readmission risk. For structured features, missing values in demographics or clinical history are imputed with column-wise means fit on the training set only.

### 2. Split Strategy: Temporal with Stratification

Data is divided temporally—reflecting realistic deployment where we train on historical data and evaluate on future data—combined with stratification to preserve class balance. The split is:

- **Training set** (months 1–36): n ≈ 30,000 patients
- **Validation set** (months 37–48): n ≈ 10,000 patients  
- **Test set** (months 49–60): n ≈ 10,000 patients

Within each time window, patients are stratified by readmission status to maintain approximately 15–20% readmission rate in each split.

**Justification**: RNN models exploit temporal sequences in patient outcomes. Using future data to train would constitute information leakage and yield optimistic estimates. A temporal split reflects the deployment scenario: the model will be trained on historical data and evaluated on patients encountered later. Additionally, hospital discharge processes, community resource availability, and clinical practice patterns evolve over time; a temporal test set captures realistic out-of-distribution conditions. Stratification ensures validation and test sets are not inadvertently imbalanced due to temporal clustering of readmissions, which would bias metric estimates.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

**Structured features**: Discharge-time age, sex, race/ethnicity, Charlson comorbidity score, admission type (emergency/planned), length of stay (days), discharge disposition, count of discharge medications, and prior 12-month readmission history. Primary diagnosis (ICD codes) is embedded using a pre-trained clinical BERT model into 768-dimensional vectors.

**Unstructured features**: Patient-reported outcomes are embedded using fine-tuned clinical BERT into fixed-length vectors. Survey responses (text) are tokenized, passed through BERT, and pooled to 768 dimensions per response.

**Sequence construction**: The RNN model operates on sequences of four timesteps: discharge (t=0) and three post-discharge survey timepoints (t=7, 14, 21 days). Each timestep contains structured features (dense-embedded) concatenated with embedded patient-reported outcomes. Sequences shorter than four timesteps are padded with zeros; longer sequences are truncated.

**Preprocessing pipeline**: All scaling (z-score normalization), encoding (one-hot for categorical features), and imputation transformers are fit exclusively on the training set (months 1–36). These fitted transformers are applied without modification to validation and test sets. This prevents future data from influencing the preprocessing parameters, eliminating a subtle source of leakage.

### 4. Model and Baseline

**RNN Model**: A bidirectional LSTM (BiLSTM) with two stacked layers (128 and 64 units) processes the feature sequences. A static context vector (dense layer, 32 units) encodes discharge-time structured features; this context is concatenated with sequential features at each timestep. The BiLSTM outputs are attended over using a learned attention mechanism; the attention-weighted output is passed through a final dense layer (64 units, ReLU) and sigmoid output for binary classification.

**Baseline Model**: Logistic regression trained on the same structured feature set (demographics, clinical history, comorbidities, prior readmission, diagnosis embeddings) but without sequential patient-reported outcomes. The baseline uses only the discharge-time snapshot, not post-discharge follow-up surveys. This ensures the comparison isolates the contribution of RNN architecture and sequential patient-reported outcomes.

**Tuning effort parity**: Both models receive equivalent hyperparameter optimization. The RNN is tuned via grid search over learning rate ∈ {0.0001, 0.0005, 0.001}, dropout ∈ {0.2, 0.4}, and batch size ∈ {32, 64}. Logistic regression is tuned over L2 regularization strength ∈ {0.001, 0.01, 0.1, 1.0}. All tuning is performed on the validation set (months 37–48).

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric**: Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve (AUROC). Readmission is a low-prevalence problem (≈15–20%); accuracy is misleading. AUROC is threshold-agnostic and reflects the model's ability to rank-order patients by risk, aligning with the stakeholder need to prioritize high-risk patients for interventions. Stakeholders can set their own operating point (sensitivity/specificity trade-off) based on available resources.

**Secondary metrics**:
- Precision-Recall AUC: reflects precision at varying recall levels, important in imbalanced classification.
- Sensitivity at 80% specificity: clinically interpretable point estimate; indicates true positive rate when false positive rate is constrained to 20%.
- Brier score and calibration slope: assess whether predicted probabilities reflect true readmission frequencies.
- Stratified AUROC by discharge disposition and primary diagnosis: detect model bias or generalization failures across patient subgroups.

### 6. Model Selection and Validation

Hyperparameter selection uses the validation set (months 37–48). For each hyperparameter configuration, the model is trained on the training set and evaluated on the validation set using AUROC. The configuration achieving the highest validation AUROC is selected. Once selected, the model is retrained on the combined training + validation data (to maximize training signal) and evaluated on the test set (months 49–60) exactly once. The test set is not accessed during hyperparameter search, threshold selection, or any other optimization step.

### 7. Controls for Confounds and Scope

Hospital discharge processes and community support resources are known to influence readmission independently of the model. We control for these confounds via: (1) stratified analysis by discharge disposition and neighborhood social deprivation index, revealing whether the RNN's advantage persists across contexts; (2) temporal stability checks on three non-overlapping test sub-windows, detecting performance degradation as practice patterns shift; (3) post-hoc inspection of RNN attention weights to verify learned sequences prioritize clinically meaningful signals (medication adherence, symptom monitoring) rather than confound proxies.

**Scope**: This experiment establishes whether RNNs with sequential patient-reported outcomes improve risk stratification compared to logistic regression on held-out future data. It does NOT establish causal mechanisms, clinical actionability, or whether deployment will reduce readmissions (which requires an intervention trial with randomized control assignment).
